4.state: Quantitative growth and allometry in praying mantises is an active field. Modern work measures how predatory-strike mechanics, metabolic rate and compound-eye (ommatidia) number scale with body size across instars, sexes and species, and now traces that scaling to mechanism — the Insulin/TOR pathway and newly assembled mantis genomes.
4.comparison: Przibram's instar-by-instar growth curve for a second mantis species (Tenodera, after Sphodromantis) is the direct ancestor of this comparative-scaling programme. Where he charted growth descriptively, modern studies add energetics and gene regulation — but the quantitative law of mantis growth he was reaching for is exactly what the field still pursues. His data go uncited.

6.verdict: Vindicated — positional memory is now molecular
6.state: Positional memory — how a regenerate 'knows' which structure to rebuild — is a central, increasingly molecular question in limb regeneration. Axolotl work locates it in connective-tissue cells, encoded by segment-specific histone marks (H3K27me3), Hand2 and the Prod1/Tig1 system, stabilised by a positive-feedback loop and even reprogrammable along the limb axis.
6.comparison: The heteromorphic long bone Sciacchitano, Weiss and Przibram produced by an edge-restricted cut is a striking early demonstration that regenerative pattern follows positional information, not merely the wound. The 1930 paper posed precisely the question modern axolotl genetics now answers at the level of chromatin and signalling.

8.verdict: Sleeping beauty — blastema potency now resolved
8.state: The reptilian regeneration blastema is now well characterised. Lizard-tail regrowth proceeds through a self-organising, tumour-like blastema whose fibroblasts drive Hedgehog-dependent cartilage, regulated by osteoclast–fibroblast crosstalk and even snoRNA-accelerated growth; tellingly, lizards regrow tails but not limbs, a difference traced to epithelial–mesenchymal transition.
8.comparison: Weiss's potency-testing of how determined versus plastic the lizard blastema is asks exactly what these molecular studies resolve: the blastema is only partially determined and yields an imperfect replacement (a cartilage rod, not true vertebrae). His 1930 extension of blastema-determination analysis from amphibians to a reptile is the conceptual forerunner, uncited.

15.state: How light shapes amphibian pigment pattern is now understood at the cellular level: visual input and the melatonin/circadian system regulate melanophore differentiation and melanisation, specific light wavelengths change melanin levels, and salamander colour diversity is being traced to gene expression and RNA methylation.
15.comparison: Kammerer's observation that transmitted coloured light affects the fire salamander's markings prefigures this light-to-pigment axis, but his account was phenomenological where modern work supplies the photic–melatonin–melanophore mechanism. A near-dormant early observation that current pigment-cell biology has effectively explained.

16.state: Whether the rearing environment can fix a colour pattern is the active field of developmental and transgenerational plasticity. Recent work documents environment-driven pigment plasticity and even multi-generational non-genetic inheritance (e.g. predator-induced morphology in Daphnia), while cautioning that some colour traits are strongly genetic and that 'transgenerational plasticity' needs tighter definition.
16.comparison: Przibram and Dembowski's result — that a yellow versus black larval background shifts the adult salamander's spots — is an early, clean case of environmental determination of a supposedly fixed pattern, exactly the question epigenetics now contests. The phenomenon is supported in principle; whether such induction is truly heritable remains open and debated.

22.state: Fish colour change is now resolved as both rapid redistribution and regulated synthesis of melanin: melanophore number and melanin content shift with background under hormonal (thyroid, melanocortin/alpha-MSH) and retina-driven photoneuroendocrine control, with MITF/ERK and thyroid-hormone signalling identified.
22.comparison: Kudo's measurement that the quantity of melanin itself changes during colour change — not merely its dispersal within chromatophores — is precisely the synthesis-versus-redistribution distinction now standard in pigment-cell physiology. The 1922 result is absorbed into, and confirmed by, modern melanophore biology.

35.state: Autologous ovarian-tissue transplantation is established clinical medicine: tissue is cryopreserved before cancer treatment and re-grafted (orthotopic for conception, heterotopic otherwise) to restore both hormonal function and fertility, with documented pregnancies and robot-assisted and hydrogel-encapsulated refinements; current work targets follicle survival (rapamycin, adipose-derived stromal cells).
35.comparison: Wiesner's 1923 proof that a rat's own ovary, grafted back, keeps working is the experimental seed of this now-routine procedure. What he showed in principle in the rat is, a century on, standard fertility-preservation practice in women — a textbook sleeping-beauty arc from lab curiosity to the clinic.

43.verdict: Open frontier — limits of endotherm regeneration
43.state: The modern consensus is that birds and mammals largely lost the broad regenerative capacity of fish and amphibians — a loss tied to the move onto land and to CNS/appendage selection — yet birds retain striking exceptions (regeneration of auditory hair cells), the beak is now a model of neural-crest-driven development, and differential organ regeneration is governed by pathways such as MAPK/ERK.
43.comparison: Werber and Goldschmidt's report that waterfowl regenerate beak tissue staked out exactly this still-contested frontier — how much regeneration warm-blooded vertebrates retain. A century later the question (which endotherm tissues regrow, and why most do not) is central to regenerative medicine; their early positive avian case is rarely cited but squarely on theme.

45.verdict: Extended — appendage identity is now Hox/evo-devo
45.state: Crustacean appendage regeneration is an active evo-devo field: regrowth proceeds via BMP signalling and a defined cell-and-gene network that faithfully rebuilds complex microanatomy and sensory function, while Hox genes specify the identities of serially-homologous limbs (walking legs versus feeding maxillipeds) and their evolutionary transformation.
45.comparison: Biberhofer's demonstration that the crayfish regrows one specific, specialised mouthpart-leg — the third maxilliped — is an early instance of exactly the serial-homology question Hox/evo-devo now answers: how a regenerate restores the correct identity of a single member of a repeated appendage series. The phenomenon is confirmed and mechanistically deepened; the 1905 paper is uncited.

46.state: Gastropod regeneration is an emerging and increasingly tractable field: the apple snail Pomacea is now a genetic model that fully regrows camera-type eyes, phagocytic hemocytes have been shown to be required for tentacle regeneration, water calcium governs shell regrowth, and regenerative capacity varies markedly across mollusc groups.
46.comparison: Cerny's early survey mapping which parts freshwater snails can rebuild is a direct precursor to this comparative, now-mechanistic programme. The mapping impulse he embodied — who-regenerates-what among gastropods — is precisely the modern comparative question, today pursued with genetics and cell biology rather than description.

47.verdict: Now a model — Ophryotrocha anterior regeneration
47.state: Polychaete anterior regeneration is a vigorous comparative field built on several model species (Ophryotrocha, Capitella, Platynereis, Pygospio): head-regeneration capacity varies by lineage and even with age, runs on molecular programs conserved across metazoans, and extends to germ-cell regeneration that challenges the germline–soma barrier.
47.comparison: Czwiklitzer's 1905 account of Ophryotrocha regrowing its anterior end, head structures included, is essentially the organism's debut as a regeneration animal — and Ophryotrocha is now exactly such a laboratory model (with a sequenced mitogenome). The specific finding is confirmed and the genus has become a modern model, though the BVA original goes uncited.

50.state: Craniofacial and jaw regeneration is a fast-moving frontier: salamanders (newts and axolotls) regrow lower jaws complete with bone, cartilage and teeth using programs shared with limb regeneration, zebrafish regenerate jaw-joint cartilage, and the mechanisms — EGR1-activated periosteal stem cells, BMP signalling, DPSC-derived vesicles — are being harnessed for human craniofacial tissue engineering, though full tooth regeneration remains elusive.
50.comparison: Werber's 1905 report that the lizard regrows jaw structures after their removal anticipated this field by a century, carrying reptilian regeneration beyond the familiar tail to the head — exactly where regenerative medicine now concentrates. A classic sleeping beauty: the question is intensely active (down to a 2026 newt-jaw study) while the founding observation goes uncited.

55.verdict: Themes still active — colour change & regeneration
55.state: Mantis colour change is now dissected genetically: pigment genes (Scarlet, ebony/tan), the Redboy pigment transporter and Wnt signalling govern background-matching, camouflage and sexual colour dimorphism, while insect appendage regeneration — with its developmental and reproductive trade-offs — is studied across many taxa.
55.comparison: This founding paper established the Egyptian mantis as a tractable animal for exactly the two phenomena — colour change and regeneration — that remain active mantis research a century later. It was model-organism infrastructure: the animal endures as a study system while the 1906 paper itself goes uncited.

61.state: The quantitative law of insect growth across moults — Dyar's rule and its relatives — remains under active scrutiny. Modern measurements find it holds only approximately: per-stage growth ratios vary by species and instar, and better exponential and multi-scale models have been proposed.
61.comparison: Przibram's serial growth measurements on Sphodromantis fed exactly this search for a quantitative moulting-growth law (the lineage that became 'Przibram's rule'). The enterprise is alive and the rule's limits are precisely today's question; his data are an early contribution, now uncited.

66.verdict: Explained — Hox identity and stage-dependent regen
66.state: The genetic control of appendage identity and regeneration is now mapped: the Hox gene Antennapedia governs antenna-versus-leg (and wing) identity, and regenerative capacity is strongly stage- and age-dependent across arthropods (ladybird beetles, centipedes, annelids).
66.comparison: Przibram's finding that mantis-larva antenna regenerates take a stage-dependent form — touching the heteromorphosis (antenna-to-leg) question — anticipates both the Hox control of antenna/leg identity and the stage-dependence of regeneration now documented. The 1917 observation is conceptually confirmed and molecularly explained, uncited.

75.verdict: Mainstream now — environment × sex on traits
75.state: How environment and sex jointly tune continuous traits is active eco-evo-devo: developmental temperature shapes sexual size dimorphism and appendage proportions across insects, fish and reptiles, and rodent tail morphology specifically tracks temperature, locomotion and local ecology.
75.comparison: Przibram's reading of rat tail length as a conditional ('facultative') sex character — sex-differentiated yet environmentally tunable — prefigures exactly this modern framing of environment-by-sex interactions in continuous morphology. The specific case is rarely cited, but the conceptual question is now mainstream.

76.state: Whether a developmentally induced trait is inherited is now the substantial field of transgenerational epigenetic inheritance: temperature and heat exposure can transmit phenotypes and gene-expression changes across generations in Drosophila, C. elegans and fish via epigenetic marks — though effects are often partial and reversible, and mammalian evidence remains debated.
76.comparison: Przibram's direct test — do tails lengthened or shortened by temperature pass to the offspring? — is precisely the transgenerational-plasticity experiment, run in the rat a century before the molecular era. The question is wide open and intensely studied; his Lamarckian framing was prescient even as the heritability stays contested.

77.verdict: Vindicated — the developmental basis of Allen's rule
77.state: The developmental basis of Allen's rule — temperature setting extremity proportions — is actively confirmed: developmental plasticity drives Allen's and Bergmann's rules in endotherms, temperature acts on bone and limb growth (even via DNA methylation in humans), and rodent tails are a canonical case.
77.comparison: Przibram did not merely observe the effect — he quantified relative tail length's temperature coefficient, putting a number on how strongly temperature scales extremity growth, exactly the developmental mechanism behind Allen's rule now being dissected. A quantitative early result the field has effectively vindicated, though uncited.

95.state: The thesis that the environment reaches the germ-plasm is now the field of transgenerational epigenetic inheritance: environmental stressors and toxicants can install heritable DNA-methylation epimutations, and somatic RNAs carried in extracellular vesicles can convey information to the gametes — though mammalian evidence remains inconclusive and 'somatic epigenetic resetting' is recognised as a genuine barrier.
95.comparison: Secerov's amphibian contribution to the institute's boldest thesis — that the environment can act on the hereditary substance — names exactly the question modern epigenetics investigates and contests. The framing was a century early; whether such inheritance is robust, especially in vertebrates, remains the open battleground.

99.state: Comparative invertebrate regeneration is active and increasingly phylogenetic: regenerative capacity varies widely across mollusc groups (dorid nudibranchs, cephalopods, gastropods) and is read as an ancestral aquatic property variably retained, now mapped onto genome-based mollusc phylogenies.
99.comparison: Czerny's survey of which parts freshwater snails and slugs can and cannot rebuild is an early entry in exactly this comparative who-regenerates-what programme across molluscs. The phenomenon-mapping he undertook is the descriptive ancestor of today's phylogenetic and molecular comparisons, though uncited.

102.state: Thermal adaptation of proteins is a live field with a nuanced modern picture: many ectotherms do adjust the thermal stability of muscle and contractile proteins to their temperature — seasonally and across species, via amino-acid changes, structure and cellular environment — though the degree varies and some proteins are strongly conserved.
102.comparison: Kryz's result — that the coagulation point of muscle plasma is fixed, independent of the temperature the animal lived at — staked out one side of exactly this question. Modern thermal biology largely finds the opposite (proteins often do acclimate), making his negative claim an early, now-qualified contribution to a still-open problem of how far protein thermostability tracks environmental temperature.

104.state: Environment-induced body plasticity in hermit crabs and other crustaceans is well documented: the occupied shell's shape moulds abdominal and uropod morphology and even claw asymmetry (crabs reared in non-coiled or artificial shelters develop more symmetrical bodies), and the mechanical interaction between soft body and hard shell shapes form.
104.comparison: Przibram's de-shelling experiment — showing the hermit crab's asymmetric abdomen differentiates according to the presence and shape of its shell — is an early, clean demonstration of exactly this shell-induced developmental plasticity, now confirmed and occasionally re-cited. The phenomenon stands and has been extended to mechanism.

107.verdict: On-theme — why regeneration is kept or lost
107.state: Why some lineages retain lifelong regeneration while others lose it is an active evolutionary question: regeneration is read as an ancestral aquatic property erased in many terrestrial animals by epigenetic and life-cycle changes, with continued moulting (as in house centipedes) linked to retained appendage regeneration.
107.comparison: Przibram's deliberate choice of the ever-moulting bristletails — reasoning that lifelong moulting keeps regenerative capacity alive — anticipates precisely the modern framing that moulting, metamorphosis and life cycle govern regenerative retention. His 'of more general significance' claim was apt: the bristletail case bears directly on today's comparative question, though uncited.

113.state: The broad phenomenon — environmentally induced phenotypes inherited via epigenetic marks — is a major active field, but it is carefully distinguished from true Lamarckism: reviews set strict criteria for transgenerational epigenetic inheritance, document germline environmental sensing, and stress both that mammalian evidence is inconclusive and that equating epigenetics with Lamarckism is a category error.
113.comparison: Kammerer's claim that experimentally enforced colour changes are inherited is the archetypal neo-Lamarckian assertion — later discredited amid the midwife-toad affair. Modern epigenetics vindicates the general question (acquired states can sometimes be transmitted) while rejecting his strong Lamarckian interpretation and the specific colour-inheritance claim, which remains unconfirmed.

118.verdict: Mechanism mapped — Wnt sets head/tail polarity
118.state: Axial polarity in annelid (and planarian) regeneration is now mechanistically mapped: the canonical Wnt/beta-catenin pathway specifies posterior identity and re-establishes head-versus-tail patterning, supported by conserved positional-identity genes, while head- and tail-regeneration capacities differ markedly by species and life stage.
118.comparison: Gluschkiewitsch's observation that the leech regenerates both ends but with different capacities front and back is an early statement of exactly the anterior–posterior polarity problem now solved at the level of Wnt signalling. The phenomenon is confirmed and the mechanism supplied; the 1907 paper is uncited.

120.state: Larval microbial and algal symbiosis is now a large field: insect and other aquatic larvae host environment-derived microbiomes that shape nutrition, development and survival — though the work centres on bacterial microbiomes more than the specific algal associations described here, and dragonfly larvae are studied largely as water-quality bioindicators.
120.comparison: Kammerer's 1907 note of a dragonfly larva living with a filamentous alga is an early observation of larval-symbiont association, the broad phenomenon now central to microbiome ecology. Modern work has shifted toward bacterial microbiomes, so the specific algal symbiosis he saw remains comparatively under-studied — an early thread still partly open.

123.state: Invertebrate eye and tentacle regeneration is an emerging model field: the apple snail Pomacea fully regrows complex camera-type eyes and is now genetically tractable (with genome editing), phagocytic hemocytes are required for tentacle regeneration, and cnidarian tentacle regeneration is traced to stem cells and Zic4.
123.comparison: Megusar's finding that the pond snail regrows its tentacle together with the eye it carries is an early demonstration of what is now a flagship invertebrate eye-regeneration system (the apple snail). The phenomenon is richly confirmed and mechanised; the 1907 observation is an uncited precursor.

124.verdict: Reawakening — the caudal horn is now an evo-devo locus
124.state: The silkworm's caudal horn has become a subject of evo-devo: a 2026 study shows cis-regulatory changes in the Wnt1/Wnt6 genes control the caudal-horn size difference between Bombyx mori and its wild relative, placing this larval appendage squarely in the genetics of pattern formation and the broader study of insect larval structures.
124.comparison: Megusar's report that the silkworm caterpillar regenerates its caudal horn is an early functional probe of how this very appendage is built — exactly the question modern Wnt-based evo-devo of the Bombyx caudal horn now answers genetically. A near-dormant result that current work has, almost literally, reopened on the same structure.

126.state: Internal-organ regeneration in amphibians is an active, expanding goal: salamanders and Xenopus repair lung tissue — by epithelial proliferation and, in the axolotl, organ-wide proliferation that restores lung mass within weeks — using pathways shared with mammals, as salamander regeneration research deliberately extends to non-canonical organs such as lung and heart.
126.comparison: Muftic's 1907 demonstration that amphibians regenerate lung tissue — carrying regeneration inside the body to an internal organ — anticipated precisely this now-active frontier of visceral-organ regeneration. The phenomenon is confirmed (down to 2024 Xenopus and axolotl lung studies) and is exactly where regenerative medicine looks; the original is uncited.

129.verdict: Sleeping beauty, waking — insect wing regeneration
129.state: Insect wing and appendage regeneration is a molecularly rich field centred on the Drosophila wing imaginal disc: a single wingless (Wnt) enhancer drives wing specification and regeneration, the Ras/MAPK pathway powers regenerative growth, and ecdysone coordinates regeneration with developmental timing — with even dietary induction of regeneration reported.
129.comparison: Werber's 1907 finding that a fully metamorphic beetle can regrow excised wings is an early case of exactly this question — whether and how holometabolous insects regenerate wings — now answered in molecular detail in Drosophila. A sleeping beauty whose phenomenon is vindicated and mechanised, though uncited.

130.verdict: Question resolved — regeneration closes before adulthood
130.state: Drosophila wing regeneration is now finely resolved: it occurs in the larval and early-pupal imaginal disc via apoptosis-induced proliferation, governed by factors such as Zelda and regulatory lncRNAs, but the capacity closes during the larva-to-pupa transition (by roughly 34 hours after pupariation) as growth turns non-proliferative — so the fully formed adult fly essentially cannot regenerate a wing.
130.comparison: Kammerer's experiment — can the adult fly, past all its moults, rebuild a removed wing? — probed exactly the limit modern work has pinned down: regenerative competence is confined to the disc stage and shuts off before adulthood. His framing of the question was apt; the modern answer is largely 'no, not in the imago', with the closing window mapped to the hour.

134.verdict: Vindicated — starvation as a lever on growth (now TOR/IGF)
134.state: Starvation and caloric restriction are central to growth and metabolic biology: undernutrition impairs growth-plate chondrogenesis and stunts growth via IGF-1 and nutrient signalling, TOR mediates feeding-dependent size plasticity, and starvation–refeeding triggers tissue-specific, often persistent metabolic adaptation across animals.
134.comparison: Morgulis's use of inanition as an experimental lever on the growth problem — showing that starvation halts and reverses growth — is exactly the approach that became modern caloric-restriction and nutrient-signalling biology. The phenomenon and its logic are vindicated and mechanised (TOR, IGF-1); the 1912 study is an early, uncited instance.

136.verdict: Major live field — environmental sex determination
136.state: Environmental (temperature-dependent) sex determination in reptiles is a major, mechanistically advancing field: warmer incubation overrides genetic sex in lizards and turtles via the pSTAT3–Kdm6b pathway acting on germ cells, with serious climate-change implications for wild sex ratios.
136.comparison: Secerov's reptilian contribution to 'the environment of the germ-plasm', with explicit attention to sex determination, points straight at what is now environmental and temperature-dependent sex determination — including in lacertid lizards like his own material. The environment-and-sex question he raised is a flourishing field; the early paper is uncited.

139.state: Parental-age effects on offspring are an active life-history field with strongly context-dependent results: depending on species, older mothers produce larger or smaller offspring, or none detectable, with consequences for size, telomeres, longevity and fitness — frequently nonzero and sometimes transmitted via mitochondria or epigenetics.
139.comparison: Przibram and Walther's clean negative result — freshly hatched mantises show no size increase with maternal age — is one data point in exactly this still-unsettled question. Modern work often finds effects in one direction or the other, so their null is neither confirmed nor refuted so much as absorbed into a literature showing the answer is species-dependent; their case is rarely revisited.

142.verdict: Confirmed — pupal colour plasticity now genetic
142.state: Environmentally cued pupal colour is well established and increasingly genetic: pupal colour plasticity tracks the pupation substrate and light (a predator-protection adaptation), is regulated by melanin-synthesis genes (tyrosine hydroxylase, laccase2, aristaless1), and is modulated by larval density and temperature.
142.comparison: Brecher's result that Vanessid pupae tune their colour to light and background, as in the cabbage white, is an early, clean demonstration of exactly this pupal-colour plasticity now mapped to substrate cues and pigment genes. The phenomenon is amply confirmed and mechanised; her instalment is uncited.

145.verdict: Largely vindicated — axis set by the cortex, not the furrow
145.state: Amphibian axis specification is well understood: the dorsoventral axis is set by cortical rotation (and a parallel microtubule array) shortly after fertilisation, driven by maternal determinants and beta-catenin, so the first cleavage furrow is only statistically — not causally — aligned with the body axis, a relationship that varies across taxa.
145.comparison: Przibram's finding — that the first cleavage furrow does not reliably coincide with the median plane, and the axis is fixed relative to the egg's natural pigment mark rather than the furrow — is essentially correct by modern lights: the axis is determined by cytoplasmic and cortical organisation, not the cleavage plane. His marking-based conclusion was largely right; cortical-rotation and molecular determinants later superseded the method and settled the question.

151.verdict: Now a flagship model — Pleurodeles regeneration
151.state: The Iberian ribbed newt Pleurodeles waltl is now a premier amphibian model for regeneration and development: its genome and transcriptome are sequenced, it supports transgenesis (Brainbow lineage tracing), heat-shock gene induction, muscle- and tendon-stem-cell work and immunogenetics — a fully modern molecular-genetic toolkit.
151.comparison: Koppanyi's 1924 characterisation of Pleurodeles biology and husbandry is precisely the model-organism groundwork that made all this possible — the unglamorous but essential establishment of the animal as a tractable laboratory newt. A century later it is a flagship regeneration model, while the founding biology paper goes uncited.

152.verdict: Textbook & confirmed — temperature sets tail length
152.state: Temperature setting extremity (especially tail) length is textbook developmental plasticity and an active climate-biology topic: Allen's rule is confirmed in rodents specifically for tails (warmer development → longer tails), and ambient temperature shapes appendage size across mammals and birds, with implications for climate-driven body-shape change.
152.comparison: Przibram and Wiesner's experiment — warming young rats lengthens the tail, the heat counterpart to their chemical-cooling work — is an early, direct causal demonstration of exactly the temperature-to-tail-length effect now formalised as the developmental basis of Allen's rule. The result is confirmed and canonical; the 1924 paper is uncited.

157.verdict: Vindicated — the pigment chemistry he called for is now mapped
157.state: The chemical and physiological causes of animal colouration are now thoroughly mapped: melanin via tyrosinase (eu- and pheomelanin), carotenoid-based colour and its enzymatic conversion (CYP2J19 and BDH1L turning yellow carotenoids into red ketocarotenoids), pterin pigments, and physiological colour change driven by pigment- and crystal-movement (dynein, cAMP).
157.comparison: Przibram's 1919 reframing of animal colouration as a problem of underlying chemical and physiological causes — companion to his and Brecher's tyrosinase/DOPA extract experiments — set the agenda that pigment-cell biology has since fulfilled. The demand was answered: colour is now read in terms of the very enzymes and pigments he pointed to. Largely vindicated, though the paper is uncited.

9.verdict: Vindicated — the regulative limb field, now molecular
9.state: Modern limb regeneration confirms the regulative principle Weiss invoked: a partial blastema can rebuild the whole pattern because positional identity is actively re-established — encoded in segment-specific H3K27me3, requiring dorsoventral cell contact (WNT10B, FGF2) and refined by genes such as Sall4 — though skeletal integration is imperfect.
9.comparison: Weiss's demonstration that a complete limb regenerates even from a half cross-section is an early, clean proof of a regulative 'determination field' that restores the whole from incomplete material — exactly the positional regulation now mapped molecularly in the axolotl. The concept is vindicated; his term 'field' survives as positional information.

11.verdict: Confirmed — position, not origin, dictates the organ
11.state: That a blastema's fate is dictated by the regional field it occupies, not its tissue of origin, is borne out by modern axolotl work: limb formation requires dorsoventral cell contact and signalling (WNT10B, FGF2), positional and posterior identity is set by factors such as Hand2, and ectopic or grafted cells take on the identity of their position.
11.comparison: Weiss's result — a tail-blastema placed in the limb field forms limb structures — is a striking early demonstration that a regional determination field, not the blastema's origin, dictates the organ. This is precisely the position-over-origin principle modern molecular regeneration confirms; the 1927 paper is its conceptual forerunner.

36.verdict: Foundational — limb grafting is now standard
36.state: Limb transplantation and regeneration in amphibians is a mature molecular field: developed-limb and blastema grafting are standard tools, nerve-mediated FGF signalling drives regeneration, and transgenic reporters (ZRS/Shh) and devices such as silk hydrogels extend the work toward inducing regeneration in less-regenerative species.
36.comparison: Weiss's demonstration that developed amphibian limbs can be transplanted and incorporated provided the morphological foundation for his own functional and 'resonance' studies — and for the grafting toolkit that underpins modern regeneration research. The technique he pioneered is now routine; the 1923 paper is an uncited methodological ancestor.

38.state: Steinach's core insight — that gonadal (interstitial) secretions determine secondary sex characters, and that manipulating them reverses sex — is foundational endocrinology, now molecular: gonad transplants functionally integrate into the hypothalamic–pituitary–gonadal axis, hormones drive sex reversal across taxa (the androgenic gland in crustaceans, steroid signalling in fish), and gonadal sex differentiation is mapped to specific pathways.
38.comparison: Steinach's 'puberty gland' experiments — implanting the opposite gonad to reverse or double sex characters and produce experimental hermaphrodites — are an origin point of hormonal sex-determination science. The principle is thoroughly vindicated; modern work supplies the molecules and the HPG-axis framework his organ-level experiments anticipated. (His was the most-cited paper in the corpus.)

39.verdict: Discredited claim — reassessed as history of science
39.state: Kammerer's midwife-toad (Alytes) claims are now treated largely as a cautionary episode in the history of science; recent scholarship calls for an objective reassessment of his phenotypic-plasticity work, while the substantive question — inheritance of environmentally induced traits — lives on, transformed, in epigenetics rather than in his Lamarckian interpretation.
39.comparison: Kammerer's report that environmentally enforced reproductive adaptations were inherited by Alytes descendants is the centrepiece of the neo-Lamarckian programme that collapsed with the 1926 nuptial-pad scandal. Modern biology rejects the specific claim and its Lamarckian reading; what survives is a more careful interest in his plasticity observations and the genuine, separate field of epigenetic inheritance.

41.verdict: Themes endure — mantis colour change & regeneration
41.state: Mantis biology is an active field across exactly Thomsen's themes: background-dependent colour change (now genetic — the Redboy transporter and pigment genes), colour vision and camouflage, raptorial-leg adaptation to prey, and appendage regeneration, with mantises a popular model for visually guided predation.
41.comparison: Thomsen's documentation of rearing, colour change and regeneration in praying mantises extended the institute's mantis programme, surveying the very phenomena that remain central to mantis research today. A descriptive foundation whose themes endure while the 1909 paper itself goes uncited.

42.state: Fish physiological colour change is well understood as chromatophore-based: pigment translocation and chromatophore proliferation (morphological colour change), coordinated by gap-junction communication and even dermal photoreception, allow background matching across fishes, loaches included.
42.comparison: Secerov's experiments showing the stone loach changes colour with background and light via chromatophore adjustment are an early, correct account of exactly this chromatophore-mediated background adaptation. The phenomenon is confirmed and mechanised (gap junctions, dermal photoreception, chromatophore proliferation); the 1909 paper is an uncited precursor.

44.state: The reproductive divergence of Salamandra atra and S. salamandra (maculosa) is now understood through ecology, genetics and phylogeny — the shift to live birth tracks climate and altitude (a dry-climate hypothesis) rather than Lamarckian induction — while Kammerer's broader plasticity claims receive cautious historical reassessment.
44.comparison: Kammerer's argument that the two salamanders' reproductive and morphological differences are environmentally induced, heritable adaptations is a Lamarckian/atavism claim later disputed. Modern work confirms the species genuinely differ but attributes this to genetic and climatic evolution, not inherited induction; his interpretation is rejected even as the salamanders remain a rich study system.

48.verdict: Confirmed — regeneration declines with age and stage
48.state: That regenerative capacity depends systematically on age, developmental stage and size is a well-established, active principle: across taxa (amphibians, damselfly larvae, annelids) younger stages regenerate better, capacity declines with age and through metamorphosis, and the molecular basis — a failure to activate regeneration genes, factors such as a MARCKS-like protein — is being worked out.
48.comparison: Kammerer's 1905 finding that amphibian-larva regenerative capacity scales with age, stage and body size states exactly this now-confirmed age- and stage-dependence of regeneration. The principle is mainstream and increasingly molecular; his early systematic statement is uncited.

49.state: Claw asymmetry (heterochely) and its regulation by regeneration and autotomy is an active crustacean topic: losing or removing a claw reorganises the large/small asymmetry, claw regeneration is dissected mechanistically (mud crab, mitten crab, crayfish) down to stem-cell sources, and lateralisation and closing-force asymmetry are quantified.
49.comparison: Przibram's result that removing a claw reorganises the heterochely — that regeneration governs which claw becomes dominant — is an early experimental demonstration of exactly the regeneration-and-asymmetry phenomenon now studied molecularly across decapods. Confirmed and extended; the 1905 paper is uncited.

57.state: Midwife-toad biology is now framed by genetics and climate: speciation proceeds through genetic hybrid incompatibilities and breeding phenology shifts with climate and weather — while Kammerer's claim that environmentally induced breeding changes are inherited remains unconfirmed.
57.comparison: Kammerer's experimental alteration of how and where the midwife toad and tree frog breed is the 'induction' step that fed his later, discredited inheritance claims. Modern anuran biology accepts that environment shifts breeding behaviour (e.g. climate-driven phenology) but not that such changes are heritably fixed in his Lamarckian sense; the induction is plausible, the inheritance is not.

59.verdict: Themes endure — mantis as a model for colour & regeneration
59.state: Sphodromantis remains a studied mantis: recent work shows ontogenetic colour change even without environmental cues (a seasonal programme), alongside the broader active fields of mantis colour change, camouflage and appendage regeneration.
59.comparison: Przibram's 1906 paper established the Egyptian mantis as a tractable experimental insect for colour change and regeneration — model-organism groundwork (companion to Grosser & Przibram's study). The animal endures in research on exactly these phenomena; the founding paper is uncited.

67.state: Environmentally cued pupal colouration in the cabbage white and other butterflies is established and increasingly genetic: pupal melanisation tracks the background with developmental trade-offs, and pigment-pattern genes (such as the lncRNA cortex) are being mapped.
67.comparison: Brecher's 1917 confirmation and extension of the cabbage-white pupa's environmentally cued colour adaptation is part of the foundational plasticity work the field now mechanises. The phenomenon is confirmed; the paper, like her companion pupal-colour studies, goes uncited.

69.state: Nerve-dependence of regeneration is a foundational, still-central principle: denervation blocks or attenuates amphibian limb (and even mammalian digit-tip) regeneration, and the mechanism is partly resolved — nerve-mediated FGF and anterior-gradient signalling drives the early regenerative phase.
69.comparison: Weiss's 1925 demonstration that denervated amphibian limbs fail to regenerate is one of the origin points of the entire nerve-dependence field, which remains active and is now molecular. A genuinely foundational result: the principle he established is textbook, though the paper itself is uncited in current work.

70.verdict: Vindicated — de-novo skeletal regeneration under the field
70.state: That the regenerate's skeleton forms anew from the blastema under positional control — not by templating the old skeleton — is confirmed: salamander limb regeneration rebuilds skeletal elements de novo via scale-invariant Shh/Fgf8 signalling and positional information, though integration with the stump is imperfect.
70.comparison: Weiss's result that limb regeneration is independent of the pre-existing skeleton — the new skeleton arising from the blastema under a regional determination field — is exactly the de-novo, position-controlled skeletal regeneration now documented molecularly. Vindicated; a classic in spirit, though the original goes uncited in current papers.

81.state: Neurochemical control of chromatophores is established physiology: adrenergic (noradrenaline) and cholinergic (acetylcholine) signalling, alongside hormones (alpha-MSH/MCH), drive pigment-organelle movement and colour change in fish and cephalopods — now resolved to second messengers (cAMP) and motor proteins (dynein).
81.comparison: Abolin's pharmacological experiments — specific drugs expanding or contracting specific chromatophores in the minnow — are an early demonstration of exactly this adrenergic, cholinergic and hormonal control of colour change. The phenomenon and its pharmacology are confirmed and deepened; the 1925 paper is uncited.

86.state: Like Kammerer's other inheritance claims, this rests on the now-discredited neo-Lamarckian programme; the legitimate modern descendant is epigenetic inheritance and developmental plasticity of pigmentation, carefully distinguished from true Lamarckism, with the specific salamander colour-inheritance claim unconfirmed.
86.comparison: Kammerer's report that enforced colour changes in the fire salamander are inherited is part of the body of work undone by the midwife-toad scandal. The general phenomenon — that environment can shape, and sometimes transmit, pigmentation — survives in epigenetics; his strong Lamarckian inheritance claim does not, and remains unconfirmed.

88.state: Systemic hormonal control of metamorphosis is foundational and molecular: thyroid hormone, via tissue-specific receptor subtypes, imposes the timing and coordination of metamorphic change across organs and species, aligning ontogenetic transitions with the environment.
88.comparison: Uhlenhuth's finding that a transplanted eye metamorphoses in synchrony with its host — the whole organism imposing its developmental timing on an isolated part — is an early, elegant demonstration of systemic (now known to be thyroid-hormone) control of metamorphosis. The inference is fully vindicated; modern work simply names the hormone and its receptors.

92.verdict: Partly echoed — cave eye/pigment plasticity vs evolution
92.state: The evolution and plasticity of cave animals' eyes and pigment is an active field: troglomorphic eye and pigment loss is explained mainly by natural selection and neutral mutation (with some epigenetic contribution), while light exposure can partially restore retinal and pigment development in some amphibians.
92.comparison: Kammerer's report that environmental light exposure elicits pigment and eye development in the normally pale, blind olm touches the plasticity side of cave-animal biology now studied in cavefish and tadpoles. Modern work frames eye/pigment regression chiefly through evolutionary genetics; the plasticity he saw is partly echoed, but his Lamarckian gloss is not supported.

93.state: Visually mediated colour change is established physiology: animals adjust their chromatophores to the background through visual (and dermal-photoreceptive) input, with the eyes and retina central to background matching across crustaceans and fishes.
93.comparison: Megusar's finding that crustacean colour change tracks background and light and fails in blinded animals is an early, clean demonstration of the eye-dependent, visually mediated chromatophore response now standard in colour-change physiology. Confirmed; the 1912 paper is uncited.

98.verdict: Vindicated — graft autonomy plus host modulation
98.state: Eye and retinal transplantation is now a major regenerative-medicine field: grafted retinal tissue and organoids develop substantially on their own, but the host environment modulates their integration, maturation and survival (including host–graft synapse formation) — the balance of autonomy and host control Uhlenhuth described.
98.comparison: Uhlenhuth's result that a transplanted eye develops largely autonomously while the host modulates its size through nutritive and spatial relations captures exactly that autonomy/host-integration balance central to today's retinal-transplant work; his eye-transplant programme also seeded Sperry's chemoaffinity hypothesis. The principle is vindicated, the original uncited.

110.verdict: Confirmed — gonadal control of sex characters
110.state: Gonadal and hormonal control of secondary sex characters is foundational endocrinology; in amphibians, sex determination and differentiation are now mapped to genetic (amh-type) and hormonal pathways, with the gonad's steroid output governing sexual phenotype.
110.comparison: Brescia's linking of newt secondary sex characters to gonadal control — the amphibian counterpart to Steinach's mammalian puberty-gland work — anticipates the gonad-hormone control of sexual phenotype now standard, even as modern work adds the genetic sex-determination layer. Confirmed in principle; uncited.

116.verdict: Answered — developmental genetics of pigment formation
116.state: The developmental formation of animal pigment is richly understood: pigment-cell (melanocyte and chromatophore) development and patterning are controlled by gene-regulatory networks and cis-regulatory variation, studied in chick, Xenopus and zebrafish across melanin, carotenoid and pterin pigments.
116.comparison: Winkler's analysis of the formation and origin of animal pigment is an early contribution to exactly this developmental account of pigmentation, now mapped to gene networks. The descriptive question he posed is answered molecularly; the 1910 paper — a companion to the institute's tyrosinase/DOPA work — is uncited.

119.state: This belongs to Kammerer's neo-Lamarckian salamander and midwife-toad programme, now treated as discredited; the legitimate modern question — transgenerational and epigenetic inheritance — is distinct and carefully bounded, and these specific inheritance claims are unconfirmed and historically reassessed.
119.comparison: Kammerer's report that enforced reproductive adaptations were transmitted to Salamandra offspring is among the inheritance claims undone by the 1926 scandal. The general inheritance-of-acquired-traits question survives in epigenetics; his specific Lamarckian results do not, and are reassessed as cautionary history.

125.verdict: Confirmed — beetle appendage regeneration is now a model
125.state: Beetle appendage regeneration is an active model system: ladybird beetles regenerate legs via a blastema (chiefly in the larval/pupal window), with capacity that is stage-specific and diet- and temperature-dependent and carries reproductive trade-offs, now imaged in 3D by micro-CT and dissected molecularly.
125.comparison: Megusar's survey of which beetle appendages regenerate and how is an early comparative map of exactly the coleopteran regeneration now studied mechanistically. The descriptive groundwork stands; modern work supplies the cells, genes and imaging, while the 1907 paper goes uncited.

127.state: The genetics of cat eye (and coat) colour is now resolved at the gene level: dominant blue eyes map to specific variants (PAX3 in Maine Coons, the DBE loci), sex-linked orange to an Arhgap36 deletion, with links to pigment-cell (neural-crest) development and to deafness.
127.comparison: Przibram's inheritance experiments on asymmetric, odd-eyed colour in Angora cats are an early genetic analysis of exactly the trait now mapped to specific genes and to the pigment/neural-crest pathways underlying odd-eyedness and associated deafness. The phenotypic inheritance he tracked is now molecular; the 1907 paper is uncited.

133.state: The regenerative potencies of the newt limb are now dissected molecularly: blastema-niche genes and microRNAs, Shh (via ZRS reporters) and FGF signalling — with FGF10 important in development but not adult regeneration — define which cells and signals rebuild a limb.
133.comparison: Kurz's mapping of the leg-forming potencies the developed newt limb retains — which tissues and amputation levels can still rebuild a leg — poses exactly the potency question now answered at the level of blastema cell types and signalling. The phenomenological map is the ancestor of today's molecular one; uncited.

135.state: The constant per-moult growth ratio formalised here ('Przibram's rule': roughly 2.09x mass and 1.26x linear dimension per instar) is now seen as an approximation: modern measurements find insects often violate the Dyar/Przibram constant (the silkworm among them), and the broader field of allometry seeks better exponential and geometric models.
135.comparison: This paper is the empirical origin of 'Przibram's rule' — that body size increases by a fixed ratio each moult. It remains a named reference point in growth biology, but the modern verdict is that the rule holds only approximately and varies by species and structure: a foundational first approximation, now refined. It is among the corpus's most-cited works.

148.verdict: Landmark question — but the resonance mechanism was superseded
148.state: The problem Weiss opened — how motor coordination is established when nerves reconnect to muscles — is central to modern neuromuscular control and prosthetics: rewiring motor axons into target muscles restores coordinated function, and electro-neuromuscular interfaces achieve high-fidelity control. His specific 'resonance' (myotypic specificity) hypothesis was, however, largely superseded by activity-dependent and connectional accounts — notably via his own student Sperry.
148.comparison: Weiss's finding that transplanted limbs move in exact synchrony with the host's homologous limb regardless of which nerves reach them grounded his influential resonance principle. The phenomenon — functional, coordinated reinnervation — is real and central to today's nerve-transfer and prosthetics work, but his proposed mechanism was overturned: a landmark whose question endures more than its answer.

149.state: Modern regeneration research routinely combines transplantation and regeneration in one system (the axolotl), showing that grafted limb tissue retains regenerative capacity governed by appendage-specific stem-cell programs (e.g. TGF-beta-dependent), distinct from primary-axis regeneration.
149.comparison: Weiss's demonstration that transplanted limbs retain the capacity to regenerate — uniting transplantation and regeneration in one system — anticipates the standard modern paradigm of grafting plus regeneration in the axolotl. The combined approach he pioneered is now ubiquitous; the 1924 paper is an uncited methodological forerunner.

156.state: The biochemistry of animal colouration is now thoroughly characterised: melanin synthesis (the tyrosinase pathway, transporters like slc45a2), carotenoid uptake and conversion enzymes, and pterins are mapped across model systems such as zebrafish and Xenopus.
156.comparison: Przibram and Brecher's preliminary biochemical experiments on the substances responsible for colour opened exactly the line — pigment chemistry — that modern molecular pigment-cell biology has fulfilled. The agenda is vindicated; the 1919 paper, a companion to the tyrosinase/DOPA work, is uncited.

159.state: Environmentally cued pupal colouration is established and increasingly genetic: pupal colour and melanisation track the pupation substrate and light (a predator-protection adaptation) and are regulated by pigment-synthesis genes, with developmental trade-offs.
159.comparison: Brecher's 1919 demonstration that the cabbage-white pupa's colour adapts to light and background is part of the foundational plasticity work the field now mechanises. The phenomenon is amply confirmed; this instalment, like her companion pupal-colour papers, goes uncited.

164.state: The antagonism of male and female sex hormones in shaping sexual phenotype is foundational endocrinology, now molecular: gonadal sex differentiation hinges on opposing pathways (e.g. Amh/androgen versus estrogen, with newly identified female-determining factors), and the androgen–estrogen balance governs secondary sex characters across vertebrates.
164.comparison: Steinach's artificial double-glands reproducing the effects of natural hermaphrodite glands — evidence that male and female puberty-gland secretions antagonise one another — anticipates the modern antagonistic-pathway model of sexual differentiation. The principle of opposing sex hormones is vindicated; modern work names the molecules. Uncited.

165.verdict: Refuted — no gonadal basis for sexual orientation
165.state: Modern science finds no distinctive gonadal histology associated with sexual orientation; orientation is understood as a normal human variation with complex, partly biological correlates (neurodevelopmental, genetic, familial) and is no longer pathologised — homosexuality was removed from the DSM decades ago.
165.comparison: Steinach extended his puberty-gland theory to sexual orientation, claiming a distinctive testicular histology in homosexual men — a claim later science does not support and that belongs to a now-discredited, harmful tradition of locating orientation in the gonad. Modern biology places orientation's correlates in neurodevelopment and genetics, not gonad histology; the specific claim is refuted, and the paper is of historical and cautionary interest only.

169.verdict: Loosely echoed — temperature acts on the endocrine system
169.state: The broad idea that temperature and environment act on the endocrine reproductive system is supported in modern terms: temperature influences gonadal development, sex determination and reproductive timing across animals (temperature-dependent sex determination, thermal effects on steroidogenesis) — though the early framing is dated.
169.comparison: Steinach and Kammerer's linking of elevated temperature to changes in the puberty gland and sexual maturation loosely prefigures the modern recognition that temperature acts on the endocrine and reproductive system. The specific framing ('climate and virility') is dated and the work goes uncited; only the general environment–endocrine link survives.

172.verdict: Confirmed — wavelength-specific control of pupal colour
172.state: Wavelength-specific (spectral) control of biological colour and photoresponses is well established: particular light wavelengths drive pigmentation and developmental or behavioural responses, with butterfly photoreceptor spectral tuning and pineal/retinal wavelength-dependent behaviour now characterised.
172.comparison: This fifth-part study pinned the pupal-colour response to specific spectral regions of light, controlling for other factors — an early, careful demonstration of wavelength-specific control of an environmentally cued colour, exactly the spectral-response phenomenon now mapped to photoreceptors and pigment pathways. Confirmed and refined; uncited.

174.verdict: Endures — Bateson's rule and positional duplication
174.state: Supernumerary, mirror-symmetric appendage formation (Bateson's rule) remains a touchstone in developmental biology, now understood through positional information and symmetry-breaking: confronting tissues of mismatched positional identity at a wound generate mirror-image duplications, studied in limb regeneration and appendage development.
174.comparison: Przibram's 'triple-formation at a break' — appendages regenerating as mirror-symmetric triples as a general rule across the animal kingdom — is an early articulation of Bateson's rule, the positional/symmetry principle still invoked for supernumerary limbs. The rule endures as a named concept; modern work supplies the positional-information mechanism. Conceptually alive though uncited.

3.state: Salamander skeletal and connective-tissue restitution is actively studied: newts regenerate bone, tendon (in two phases) and other skeletal tissue, with genome and transcriptome tools illuminating regeneration-specific gene expression.
3.comparison: This paper's demonstration that the newt regenerates long bones after partial internal removal — skeletal restitution within an otherwise intact limb — is an early instance of the internal skeletal regeneration salamanders are now known for and that modern molecular work dissects. Confirmed, though only lightly cited.

5.state: How the compound eye scales with body size — both ommatidial size and number — is an active topic, now traced to the Insulin/TOR pathway and developmental genetics, with 3D-reconstruction methods quantifying ommatidia across insects.
5.comparison: This study's quantification that both facet size and number increase as the mantis grows is an early, direct measurement of compound-eye scaling now pursued mechanistically (Insulin/TOR, eye-development genes). The descriptive scaling result is confirmed and deepened; lightly cited.

10.verdict: Confirmed — tissue origins of the regenerate, now lineage-traced
10.state: The tissue origins of the limb regenerate are now traced with lineage tools: regeneration draws on defined cell sources for each tissue (the epidermis from wound epithelium), with shared and derived mechanisms across axolotl, zebrafish and Polypterus fins.
10.comparison: Weiss's establishment of the origin of the skin in the limb regenerate — clarifying which tissue supplies the new epidermis — is an early version of the lineage question now answered by modern fate-mapping (the wound epidermis as epidermal source). The question he posed is standard in regeneration biology; the 1927 paper is uncited.

12.verdict: Confirmed — neonatal rodents track ambient temperature
12.state: Developmental thermoregulation in rodents is well characterised: neonatal rodents are not fully homeothermic and depend on ambient temperature — a fact central to modern lab-animal practice (housing near thermoneutrality, ~28-29°C, to avoid cold stress) and to neonatal thermal-physiology research.
12.comparison: de Hahn's finding that young brown rats are not yet fully homeothermic, their body temperature tracking the environment, states exactly the neonatal-thermoregulation fact now standard in rodent physiology and husbandry. Confirmed and modestly cited — and it underpins the institute's own rat tail-length-versus-temperature experiments.

14.state: Environmentally cued pupal colouration in the cabbage white is established and increasingly genetic: pupal colour tracks pupation substrate and light as a predator-protection adaptation, regulated by melanin-synthesis genes with developmental trade-offs.
14.comparison: Brecher's 1922 result that the cabbage-white pupa matches its colour to the surrounding light and background is a clean instance of exactly this plasticity, now mapped to substrate cues and pigment genes. One of her several pupal-colour papers in the corpus; confirmed and uncited.

17.state: Environmentally cued pupal colouration is established and increasingly genetic across butterflies: pupal colour tracks substrate and light as a predator-protection adaptation, regulated by pigment genes (including the lncRNA cortex), with developmental trade-offs.
17.comparison: Przibram and Brecher's result that Vanessid pupae match their colour to light and background, as the cabbage white does, is one of the institute's several demonstrations of this now-confirmed plasticity. The phenomenon is mainstream and increasingly molecular; the paper is uncited.

18.verdict: Resolved in principle — the PD axis is determined yet reprogrammable
18.state: The proximo-distal axis of the regenerating limb is now understood as encoded positional information that is normally stable but can be re-specified: retinoic acid proximalises blastema cells and factors such as Prod1/Tig1 and Hand2 set positional identity, so PD identity is firmly 'determined' yet experimentally reversible.
18.comparison: Kurz's polarity-reversal experiments — testing whether limb polarity is fixed or reversible, a direct probe of how firmly the proximo-distal axis is determined — pose exactly the positional-determination question modern axolotl work answers: the axis is robustly determined but can be reprogrammed. The question is central and resolved in principle; this paper is uncited (and Consensus retrieval for it was thin).

20.verdict: Vindicated — pupation is a committed hormonal (ecdysone) signal
20.state: The hormonal control of pupation is well understood: the steroid 20-hydroxyecdysone, with the brain's PTTH and juvenile hormone, triggers and times metamorphosis; once the critical period passes and the hormonal cascade is committed, pupation proceeds independently of the head and brain.
20.comparison: Przibram's finding that caterpillars decapitated after a critical period still pupate — the trigger having been released into the body and no longer needing the head — is an early, elegant demonstration of exactly this: a circulating hormonal signal (now known to be ecdysone, downstream of brain PTTH) commits the animal to metamorphosis. The 'critical period' he identified is a real endocrine threshold; vindicated, with the hormone since named. Uncited.

21.state: Environmentally cued pupal colouration is established and increasingly genetic: pupal colour tracks pupation substrate and light, is regulated by melanin-synthesis genes and the lncRNA cortex, and shows developmental trade-offs across butterflies.
21.comparison: Brecher's extension of the cabbage-white result to the Vanessids — pupae adapting their colour to light and background — broadened the evidence for exactly this plasticity, now mapped to substrate cues and pigment genes. Confirmed and mainstream; the paper is uncited.

24.verdict: Foundational method — autografting is now standard
24.state: Autologous (autoplastic) grafting is now a cornerstone of regenerative medicine and surgery: autografts avoid immune rejection and are used for skin, soft-tissue and organ reconstruction, with modern refinements such as micro-column harvesting, scaffold-guided regeneration and stem-cell-derived grafts.
24.comparison: Przibram's codification of the autoplastic transplantation method — grafting tissue back onto the same animal as a controlled tool for regeneration and potency studies — anticipates the central role of autografts in both experimental regeneration and clinical reconstruction, precisely because same-animal grafts avoid rejection. The method is foundational and ubiquitous; the 1923 paper is uncited.

25.verdict: Live frontier — eye replantation, optic nerve the bottleneck
25.state: Eye replantation and transplantation is a live frontier: allogeneic whole-eye transplantation is now surgically feasible with partial promise, but functional vision restoration is limited by optic-nerve (retinal ganglion cell axon) regeneration — the central unsolved bottleneck.
25.comparison: Koppanyi's 1923 demonstration that eyes can be replanted and re-establish structure and some function is an early instance of exactly the whole-eye transplantation now pursued in medicine — and the limit he would have met, reconnecting the optic nerve, remains the field's key obstacle. The phenomenon is confirmed and the modern frontier echoes his result; his eye work also fed Sperry's chemoaffinity studies. Uncited.

27.verdict: Live frontier — eye replantation / ocular regeneration
27.state: Eye replantation and transplantation is a live frontier: whole-eye transplantation is now surgically feasible, and ocular-surface and retinal regeneration therapies (cell-based, growth-factor, tissue-engineered) are advancing — but functional recovery is limited by optic-nerve (retinal ganglion cell) regeneration.
27.comparison: This second, physiology-focused part of Koppanyi's eye-replantation series further documents that a replanted eye recovers structure and some function — the autonomy-plus-partial-function result echoed in today's whole-eye transplantation and ocular regeneration work, with optic-nerve reconnection the persistent limit. Uncited.

28.state: Whole-eye transplantation has reached feasibility milestones: allogeneic transplants in macaques achieve graft survival with preserved structure, and combined whole-eye-and-facial transplantation is being explored for catastrophic trauma — though long-term viability and optic-nerve regeneration remain unsolved.
28.comparison: Koppanyi's confirmation that a replanted eye can survive and partly recover directly prefigures modern whole-eye (and eye-plus-face) transplantation now achieving graft survival. The phenomenon is borne out; the obstacle he would have met — restoring the optic-nerve connection — is still the field's central challenge. Uncited.

34.verdict: Confirmed — lens tolerates transplantation; lens regeneration now active
34.state: Lens biology and regeneration is active: the lens can regenerate (notably via pigment-epithelial/Wolffian regeneration in newts and via lens-epithelial stem cells), and ocular stem-cell and transplantation approaches target lens, cornea and retina repair.
34.comparison: Wiesner's demonstration that the adult crystalline lens can be replanted and survive — the lens tolerating transplantation like the whole eye — is an early result on lens graftability that complements the lens-regeneration biology now central to ocular regenerative medicine. Confirmed in spirit; lightly cited.

37.verdict: Foundational — limb grafting is now standard
37.state: Amphibian limb transplantation and regeneration is a mature molecular field: developed-limb and blastema grafting are standard tools, nerve-mediated FGF signalling drives regeneration, and transgenic reporters (ZRS/Shh) extend the work toward inducing regeneration elsewhere.
37.comparison: This second part of Weiss's developed-limb transplantation work provided the surgical foundation for his functional and field studies — and for the grafting toolkit now routine in regeneration research. The technique is ubiquitous today; the 1923 paper is an uncited methodological ancestor.

40.state: Fish physiological colour change is well understood as chromatophore-based: pigment translocation and chromatophore adjustment, under neural, hormonal and visual control, allow background matching and camouflage across fishes.
40.comparison: Mayerhofer's pike experiments showing colour change with background and light via chromatophore adjustment are an early, correct account of exactly this chromatophore-mediated background adaptation, now mechanised. Confirmed; uncited.

51.verdict: Confirmed — functional sense-organ regeneration in insects
51.state: Sensory-organ (antenna and eye) regeneration in arthropods is studied and shown to restore function: regenerated antennae retain sensory functionality (e.g. in isopods), and insect appendage and sense-organ regeneration runs on redeployed developmental programmes.
51.comparison: Werber's finding that the mealworm beetle regenerates excised sense organs — antenna and eye — within its moulting cycle is an early demonstration of functional sensory-organ regeneration in a holometabolous insect, the phenomenon now confirmed (regenerated arthropod antennae are functional) and dissected developmentally. Uncited.

52.state: Jaw and craniofacial regeneration is an active comparative field: salamanders (newts and axolotls) regrow lower jaws complete with bone, cartilage and teeth using limb-regeneration-like programmes, while amniotes largely cannot — giving exactly the cross-group differences in regenerative extent.
52.comparison: Werber's 1906 comparison — that both reptiles and amphibians regenerate jaw structures, with differences in extent between the groups — anticipates the modern comparative picture (amphibians regenerate jaws richly, reptiles and amniotes less so). The phenomenon and the cross-group difference are confirmed; uncited, and a companion to his lizard-jaw sleeping-beauty paper.

54.verdict: Confirmed — fin regeneration is now a flagship model
54.state: Fish fin regeneration is a premier regeneration model: zebrafish and Polypterus regrow fins via a blastema dependent on BMP and FGF signalling, with fin rays carrying a 'remembered' positional identity — shared and derived mechanisms with tetrapod limb regeneration.
54.comparison: Bogacki's demonstration that European freshwater fishes regenerate amputated fins with characteristic regrowth patterns is an early description of exactly the fin regeneration now central to the field, down to positional memory and BMP/FGF control. Confirmed and richly mechanised; lightly cited.

56.verdict: Foundational — Hydra model and algal symbiosis
56.state: Hydra is a flagship model for regeneration, patterning and symbiosis: its epithelial stemness powers whole-body regeneration, and the green-hydra–Zoochlorella symbiosis is studied as a model 'metaorganism' (algal recognition and uptake, symbiont effects on the microbiome and energy balance).
56.comparison: Hadzi's preliminary biology of Hydra — documenting the green hydra's Zoochlorella symbiosis and basic feeding and biology as groundwork for an experimental biology of the polyp — is exactly the model-organism foundation that made Hydra a cornerstone of regeneration and symbiosis research. The symbiosis he described is now a studied metaorganism; the 1906 paper is uncited.

60.verdict: Prescient analogy — echoed in biomineralisation & self-assembly
60.state: The crystal–organism analogy survives in two modern guises: biomineralisation (organisms control crystal growth — biogenic guanine crystals, crystallisation by particle attachment) and the physics of self-assembly and symmetry-breaking, where crystal growth, regeneration after breakage, and symmetry are actively studied.
60.comparison: Przibram's 'crystal analogies' — using crystals as physical models for organic growth, regeneration and symmetry, a reductionist frame for Entwicklungsmechanik — anticipates both modern biomineralisation (organisms literally growing controlled crystals) and the physics-of-life view that growth and symmetry obey general self-organising principles. The analogy was prescient and is echoed (crystals even 'regenerate' after breakage); the 1906 essay is an uncited conceptual ancestor.

62.verdict: Prescient — an early physics of animal movement (active matter)
62.state: Treating animal movement as a stochastic, physical process is now the field of active matter and collective behaviour: random-walk and Poisson models describe microbial and insect navigation, and collective motion (locusts, fish) is modelled as order/disorder transitions arising from local stochastic interactions.
62.comparison: Karl Przibram — the physicist brother — describing the movement of lower organisms as a disordered, quasi-random process, a physics of behaviour, is a strikingly early instance of what is now active-matter and stochastic-movement science. The conceptual move (apply statistical physics to behaviour) is exactly today's framework; the 1917 paper is an uncited forerunner.

63.state: How temperature scales physiological rates is a central, formalised field: thermal performance curves (and proposals for a 'universal' TPC), Q10/Arrhenius relations and aerobic-scope concepts quantify temperature's effect on development, metabolism and survival across animals.
63.comparison: Przibram's determination of temperature quotients for the mantis's vital processes is an early quantitative thermal-physiology measurement — exactly the temperature-scaling of rates now formalised in thermal performance curves and Q10 analysis. The quantitative approach is vindicated and standard; the 1917 paper is uncited.

64.verdict: Vindicated in principle — temperature reaches the germ line
64.state: The idea that the environment, notably temperature, reaches the germ line is now substantiated in specific ways: temperature affects spermatogenesis and gametes, thermosensory neurons regulate germline transposons and heritable expression (in C. elegans), and thermal exposure alters germline epigenetic marks in fish — a real, if bounded, channel for environmental effects on heredity.
64.comparison: Przibram's thesis that the environment — especially temperature — penetrates to and modifies the germ-plasm names exactly this now-active channel. His framing is vindicated in principle (temperature does reach, and can mark, the germ line); the magnitude and true heritability remain the contested frontier. Uncited.

65.verdict: Confirmed — fiddler-crab claw development and regeneration
65.state: Fiddler-crab claw development and regeneration is studied behaviourally and molecularly: the great claw's form has ecological consequences (mating success, density effects), and decapod claw regeneration is increasingly dissected at the gene level.
65.comparison: Przibram's documentation that the fiddler crab's great claw passes through characteristic transitory forms — a developmental sequence revealed in regeneration — is an early morphological account of exactly the claw development and regeneration now studied molecularly and ecologically. The descriptive sequence stands; lightly cited.

71.verdict: Confirmed — the reach of the regenerative field
71.state: The spatial reach and cellular basis of the urodele limb's regenerative field is well characterised: blastema formation can be induced from limb surfaces, dermal fibroblasts are a key multipotent source, and signalling (even induced regeneration via factor cocktails) is being mapped.
71.comparison: Weiss's finding that the urodele limb can regenerate from its lateral surfaces — revealing the spatial reach of its regenerative field — probes exactly the extent and source of the blastema-forming field now studied molecularly (dermal-fibroblast contribution, induced lateral regeneration). Consistent with modern findings; lightly cited.

72.verdict: Confirmed — quantitative wing morphometrics, now automated
72.state: Quantitative wing morphometrics is a standard, now-automated field: geometric morphometrics and computer-vision tools (such as WingAnalogy) measure wing shape, asymmetry and their adaptive and seasonal variation across butterflies and other insects.
72.comparison: Weiss's angle measurements on the butterfly wing — treating wing form as measurable morphometric data — prefigure modern quantitative wing morphometrics, now automated and applied to adaptation and asymmetry. The morphometric approach he took is mainstream; lightly cited.

73.verdict: Vindicated — Hydra head/foot polarity, now Wnt organisers
73.state: Hydra axial determination is a model for patterning: head and foot organisers run on Wnt/beta-catenin gradients (Wnt promotes the head and activates foot regeneration), reinforced by mechano-chemical feedback (tissue strain, actin-fibre organisation) and epigenetic regulation (H4K20me1), with foot and head behaving as distinct polar systems.
73.comparison: Burt's finding that the head and foot of Hydra behave as unipotent systems, each regenerating along its own polarity, is an early statement of the axial determination now mechanised as opposing Wnt-based head/foot organisers with mechano-chemical feedback. The polar-organiser concept is vindicated; the 1925 paper is uncited.

74.verdict: Confirmed — temperature sets tail length (Allen's rule)
74.state: Temperature directly setting extremity and tail length is textbook developmental plasticity and an active climate-biology topic: Allen's rule is confirmed in rodents specifically for tails (warmer development → longer tails), with implications for climate-driven body-shape change.
74.comparison: Przibram's direct experimental demonstration that rearing temperature sets tail length — warmer rats growing longer tails — is the same temperature-to-proportion effect now formalised as the developmental basis of Allen's rule, with rodent tails the canonical case. Confirmed and canonical; lightly cited, and a companion to his other rat-tail temperature papers.

78.verdict: Vindicated — the DOPA test for melanogenesis
78.state: The DOPA reaction validated here underlies modern histochemistry of melanogenesis: tyrosinase acting on tyrosine and DOPA within melanosomes is the established melanin-synthesis pathway, now studied genetically (slc45a2, tyrosinase) across zebrafish, mammals and beyond.
78.comparison: Sato and Brecher's demonstration that the DOPA reaction agrees between frozen sections and extracts — confirming the histochemically detected enzyme is the same one assayed biochemically — validated the DOPA test for locating melanin formation, a method foundational to a century of pigment-cell histochemistry. The validation holds; the enzyme and pathway are now molecular. Uncited.

79.verdict: Vindicated — tyrosine/DOPA as the melanin substrate
79.state: The substrate question for vertebrate melanin is firmly answered: tyrosinase converts tyrosine to DOPA to dopachrome to melanin — the canonical pathway, now mapped genetically and structurally across vertebrates.
79.comparison: This companion study, asking whether DOPA or tyrosine yields the chromogen in vertebrates and confirming the section/extract agreement, probed the substrate of vertebrate melanin synthesis — now firmly established as tyrosine and DOPA acted on by tyrosinase. Vindicated; uncited.

80.verdict: Confirmed — timing of DOPA/melanisation, now genetic
80.state: The timing and genetics of insect melanin-precursor (DOPA) production is well resolved: tyrosine hydroxylase and DOPA decarboxylase (DDC) control DOPA and dopamine for cuticular melanisation (DDC knockout causes albinism), with stage-specific deployment during larval-to-pupal transitions.
80.comparison: Sciacchitano's pinpointing of when DOPA — the melanin precursor — forms during cocoon construction is an early timing study of exactly the DOPA/melanisation pathway now mapped to specific enzymes (TH, DDC) and their developmental timing in insects. The temporal-control question is answered genetically; uncited.

82.verdict: Vindicated — limb three-axis patterning, now molecular
82.state: The three-axis organisation and developmental potencies of the limb — the framework Harrison's grafting experiments opened — is now molecular: the proximodistal (Shh/FGF), anteroposterior and dorsoventral axes of the limb bud and blastema are specified by defined signalling centres and morphogen gradients.
82.comparison: Przibram's framing of how the urodele limb's three axes and developmental potencies are organised — interpreting Harrison's transplantation experiments — is an early conceptual systematisation of limb-axis determination, exactly the problem now resolved into Shh/FGF/Wnt axis-patterning. The scaffold he built on Harrison's work is vindicated by molecular axis biology; uncited.

83.verdict: Thesis vindicated, inheritance claim not — plasticity vs Lamarckism
83.state: That external conditions drive organic (phenotypic) variation is now the well-developed field of phenotypic plasticity and eco-evo-devo: the environment shapes development and variation through mechanisms from mechanical stress in growing tissues to gene-environment interaction.
83.comparison: Kammerer's presentation of external living conditions as a driver of organic variation — the theoretical core of his environmental programme — anticipates the modern consensus that the environment is a major source of phenotypic variation. The general thesis is vindicated; his specific Lamarckian claim that such variation is heritably fixed is not. (Consensus retrieval here was noisy, matching 'organic' in unrelated senses.)

84.verdict: Vindicated — graded DV potency, now morphogen gradients
