84.state: That morphogenetic capacity is regionally graded along body axes is foundational and molecular: morphogen gradients (Dpp/BMP, Shh) pattern the dorsoventral axis, now demonstrated even in organoids (dorsoventral spinal-cord and brain patterning by localised morphogens).
84.comparison: Przibram's finding that form-building capacity is unevenly distributed along the dorso-ventral axis — morphogenetic potency regionally graded — is an early statement of exactly the graded, axis-dependent developmental potential now explained by morphogen gradients (BMP/Shh) patterning the DV axis. The concept is vindicated and mechanised; uncited.

85.state: Homeotic regeneration — an ordered but wrong organ regenerating in place of another — is understood as Hox and positional misspecification; in insects, antenna-versus-eye and antenna-versus-leg identity is governed by developmental selector genes, and regenerated sense organs such as antennae are functional.
85.comparison: Janda's report that an antenna-like organ regenerates in place of a removed eye — a classic homeotic heteromorphosis — is an early instance of the phenomenon now framed by Hox/selector-gene control of organ identity. Confirmed and conceptually explained; uncited.

87.verdict: Imaginative analogy — cross-taxon growth laws, not universal
87.state: Foraminiferal chamber growth is now quantitatively studied (automated 3D growth-trajectory reconstruction), and cross-taxon growth-law comparisons remain a theme in allometry — though a single universal law spanning foraminifera and mantids is not a modern claim.
87.comparison: Przibram's striking analogy — that foraminiferan shell-chamber addition follows a progression like the mantid moulting series, a shared quantitative growth law across distant taxa — exemplifies his search for universal growth laws. Modern foraminiferal growth is quantified and cross-taxon scaling is studied, but the specific universal-law claim is not pursued; an imaginative early comparison, uncited.

91.state: Annelid (earthworm) reproductive-organ and whole-body regeneration is an active model field: earthworm prostate and clitellum systems are used to study regeneration and stem cells, and annelids regenerate complex structures — including germ cells — challenging the germline–soma barrier.
91.comparison: Janda's demonstration that the earthworm regenerates its reproductive organs — an unusually demanding restitution of a whole organ system — is an early instance of exactly the annelid reproductive-organ and germ regeneration now studied with earthworm prostate models and clitellum stem cells. Confirmed and mechanistically pursued; uncited.

94.verdict: Live & contested — environment reaches the germ line
94.state: The germ line is now known to be influenced by the environment — its epigenome altered by stress, pollution and temperature — reopening the question of environmental effects on heredity, though robustly bounded and debated.
94.comparison: Przibram's thesis that the germ-plasm is open to its environment — a channel for environmental effects on heredity — is the conceptual seed of transgenerational epigenetic inheritance, where environmental signals can mark the germline epigenome. It is vindicated as a live question (a 2016 epigenetics review cites this very paper as a Przibram/Steinach precedent); the strength and heritability remain contested.

96.verdict: Live & contested — environment as a factor in heredity
96.state: Whether the surroundings of the germ plasm influence heredity is the active field of transgenerational epigenetics: temperature, heat and pollution can alter the germline epigenome and gamete development, providing a real channel for environmental effects on inheritance.
96.comparison: Congdon's treatment of the germ plasm as influenced by its surroundings — environment as a factor in heredity — is, like the institute's other germ-plasm papers, an early articulation of what is now transgenerational epigenetics. The general channel is supported; the specific heritability claims remain bounded and debated. Uncited.

97.verdict: Answered — colour change is both skin-local and systemic
97.state: Fish colour change is understood as both locally and systemically controlled: chromatophores respond to local, neural and hormonal signals, and skin-pigment patterning has cell-autonomous genetic components — so the response is partly skin-intrinsic and partly imposed by the whole animal.
97.comparison: Secerov's skin-graft-plus-colour-change experiments — testing whether the loach's colour response is local to the skin or imposed by the whole animal — pose exactly the autonomy question modern pigment biology resolves as 'both'. His experimental logic of using grafts to separate local from systemic control is the right one; uncited.

100.verdict: Largely bypassed — an extreme intergeneric cross, uncorroborated
100.state: Fish hybridisation is a well-studied phenomenon in ecology and evolution (natural hybridisation, introgression, hybrid viability), but the modern emphasis is on hybridisation within and between closely related species and its genomic consequences, not on forced crosses between distant genera.
100.comparison: Kammerer's claim that the river perch and ruffe — different genera — can be hybridised to yield intermediate offspring is a bold early hybridisation experiment that modern fish biology does not corroborate and treats such distant intergeneric crosses skeptically. The specific result is uncited; Consensus retrieval found little modern engagement with this exact claim (mostly perch ecology and toxicology).

106.verdict: Themes endure — mantis colour change & regeneration
106.state: Mantis biology remains active across exactly these themes: background-dependent and ontogenetic colour change (now genetic — the Redboy transporter and pigment genes), sexual colour dimorphism, camouflage, and appendage regeneration.
106.comparison: Przibram's establishment of the European mantis, like its Egyptian counterpart, as a rearable experimental insect for colour change and regeneration is model-organism groundwork whose themes endure in current mantis research. The animal persists as a study system; the 1907 paper was cited by contemporaries (Megušar and others) but is uncited today.

108.verdict: Vindicated — the enzymatic (tyrosinase) theory of pigment
108.state: The enzymatic theory of pigmentation is established and molecular: tyrosinase, with the tyrosinase-related proteins TYRP1/2, initiates and controls melanin biosynthesis (TYRP1 even requiring zinc), now characterised genetically and biochemically — tyrosinase has even been used to make synthetic melanin.
108.comparison: Weindl's 1907 proposal that pigment arises through pre-formed tyrosinase enzymes — an enzymatic theory of animal pigmentation — is essentially correct and foundational: tyrosinase is indeed the melanin-forming enzyme, exactly as the institute's DOPA/tyrosinase programme proposed. Strongly vindicated; uncited.

109.state: Spider leg autotomy and regeneration is actively studied behaviourally and ecologically: spiders shed legs defensively and compensate functionally — rapidly adopting new gaits and recovering speed — while regeneration restores legs over moults.
109.comparison: Otto Weiss's finding that the water spider sheds legs by autotomy and regenerates them — linking a defensive reflex to regenerative capacity — is an early statement of exactly this autotomy-plus-regeneration biology now studied for its adaptive value and functional (gait) compensation. Confirmed; uncited.

111.state: Crustacean colour change is well characterised: prawns adjust their chromatophores (and pigments such as crustacyanin) to the background, with chromatophore number increasing on dark backgrounds and behavioural background-matching, under hormonal control.
111.comparison: Fröhlich's early analysis of the prawn's colour-change reactions is a founding description of exactly the crustacean chromatophore control now dissected — crustacyanin, hormonal regulation and background-dependent chromatophore number in Palaemon and its relatives. Confirmed and deepened; uncited.

112.verdict: Real but context-dependent — maternal-age effect on size
112.state: Maternal-age effects on offspring size are an active but context-dependent field: older mothers may produce larger or smaller offspring depending on species, with sex-specific effects and mechanisms (including mitochondrial transmission) — no single universal direction.
112.comparison: Halban's finding that egg and newborn size rise with the mother's age states one direction of the maternal-age effect now known to be real but species- and context-dependent (sometimes the reverse). His positive result is one data point in a literature that finds the effect genuine but variable; uncited — and a useful contrast to the institute's own mantis null result.

114.verdict: Confirmed — specialized leg regeneration in arthropods
114.state: Regeneration of specialised arthropod legs is well studied: different leg types regenerate (mostly in the larval/nymphal window, over moults) with functional recovery such as gait compensation, alongside specialisation like the raptorial foreleg — now approached molecularly.
114.comparison: Megusar's documentation of how the differently specialised orthopteran legs (grasping, walking, jumping) regenerate when the animals are reared from young is an early comparative survey of exactly this specialised-appendage regeneration now studied functionally and molecularly across arthropods. Confirmed; uncited.

115.state: Homeosis — the substitution of one body part for another — is a foundational concept of evo-devo, explained by Hox and selector genes; homeotic transformations (including in arthropod appendages, and even intracellular homeosis in ciliates) remain a standard analytic window on development and evolution.
115.comparison: Przibram's synthesis 'Homoeosis in Arthropods' — establishing homeosis as a regular, analysable phenomenon frequently revealed through regeneration — helped systematise a concept (homeosis, after Bateson) that became central to evo-devo and Hox-gene biology. The concept is foundational and thriving; the 1910 paper is an uncited ancestor of homeotic-transformation research.

117.verdict: Vindicated — the contractile vacuole as osmoregulator
117.state: The contractile vacuole is established as the protist osmoregulatory organelle: its pulsation tracks the osmotic load (slowing or stopping in high-osmolarity media) and it expels water via membrane tubes, ion pumps and cytoplasmic pressure — now studied structurally and molecularly.
117.comparison: Zuelzer's finding that seawater slows or stops the contractile vacuole, its pulsation tracking the osmotic load, is an early and correct demonstration of the organelle's osmoregulatory function, exactly as modern cell biology confirms. The inference is vindicated; uncited.

121.verdict: Confirmed — regeneration of hormone-dependent sex characters
121.state: Both amphibian regeneration and the hormonal control of secondary sex characters are well established; their intersection — regrowth of sex-related structures under endocrine control — fits modern endocrinology and regeneration, with androgens, estrogens and thyroid hormone governing such characters.
121.comparison: Kammerer's finding that amphibians regenerate their secondary sexual characters — linking regeneration to the sex-character (hormonal) system — connects two now-mature fields; modern work confirms both the regeneration of such structures and their endocrine control. Confirmed in principle; uncited.

128.state: Claw-asymmetry reversal after loss is established crustacean biology: losing the major claw triggers the remaining minor claw to transform into the major while the regenerate becomes the minor — a reversal now studied for the developmental and molecular basis of claw laterality, and inducible by substrate and use.
128.comparison: Przibram's 'claw reversal' — after losing the large claw, the small claw takes over as large and the regenerate becomes small — is an early, precise description of exactly this handedness-reversal now confirmed and studied mechanistically in lobsters and crayfish. Confirmed and extended; cited by contemporaries (Kammerer), uncited today.

131.state: The coupling of regeneration and growth is an active theme: regeneration reactivates developmental and growth programmes (Hox 'rebooting', morphogen-gradient scaling for proportional regrowth), and regenerative growth can alter overall growth dynamics.
131.comparison: Zuelzer's finding that regeneration accelerates the isopod's growth rate is an early empirical case for the regeneration-as-growth link — the idea, central to the institute's programme (Przibram's 'regeneration as growth-acceleration'), that regeneration taps the growth machinery. Modern work confirms regeneration reactivates growth and developmental programmes; uncited.

132.state: Annelid (earthworm) reproductive-organ and whole-body regeneration is an active model field: earthworm prostate and clitellum systems are studied ex vivo, reproductive organs emerge in stereotyped sequences with body size, and immature worms regenerate comparably to adults.
132.comparison: Part I of Janda's earthworm work confirms and details the capacity to regrow reproductive organs — exactly the annelid reproductive and germ regeneration now studied with modern models. Confirmed; uncited (companion to Part II).

138.verdict: Vindicated — systemic (thyroid) control of gill resorption
138.state: Metamorphic gill resorption is now known to be driven systemically by thyroid hormone (locally fine-tuned by deiodinases) rather than by the gill's autonomous programme — gill resorption proceeds as part of coordinated, hormone-timed organ remodelling during metamorphosis.
138.comparison: Kornfeld's demonstration that metamorphic gill-resorption depends on the whole organism — a systemic signal, not the gill's own programme — is an early, correct inference of the systemic (now known to be thyroid-hormone) control of metamorphic organ resorption. Vindicated; modern work names the hormone and the deiodinase timing. Uncited.

140.verdict: Confirmed approach — instar growth; the constant ratio is approximate
140.state: Instar-by-instar growth measurement is a standard method in insect biology, but the constant per-moult growth ratio (Dyar's/Przibram's rule) is now seen as only approximate, varying with species, diet and instar.
140.comparison: Sztern's instar-by-instar quantification of the mantis growth curve adds to the institute's biometric growth programme (the 'Przibram's rule' lineage). Modern work keeps instar-based growth measurement as standard but finds the constant-ratio rule holds only roughly. Confirmed approach; uncited.

141.state: Environmentally cued pupal colouration in the cabbage white is established and increasingly molecular: pupal colour tracks substrate and light (a predator-protection adaptation), regulated by pigment-synthesis genes, with pupal development under hormonal (ecdysone) control.
141.comparison: Brecher's 1924 cabbage-white study establishing the pupa's environmentally cued colour adaptation is one of her several papers on this now-confirmed plasticity, mapped today to pigment genes and ecdysone-timed pupal development. Confirmed; uncited.

143.state: Homeotic antenna-to-leg regeneration is understood through Hox and selector-gene control of appendage identity, with the conditions (amputation level, positional value) determining whether a leg or antenna regenerates fitting the positional-information framework.
143.comparison: Brecher's careful definition of the conditions — notably amputation level — under which the stick insect Dixippus regenerates a leg where an antenna belongs is a controlled study of homeotic heteromorphosis, exactly the antenna/leg identity switch now explained by Hox genes and positional value. This paper itself cites Przibram's 1919 homeosis work; explained today, but uncited.

146.verdict: Vindicated — limb three-axis patterning, now molecular
146.state: The three-axis organisation and developmental potencies of the limb — the framework Harrison's grafting opened — is now molecular: the proximodistal (Shh/FGF), anteroposterior and dorsoventral axes are specified by defined signalling centres and morphogen gradients.
146.comparison: This 1924 companion frames the urodele limb's three axes and potencies, interpreting Harrison's grafting results — the conceptual systematisation of limb-axis determination now resolved into molecular axis-patterning. Vindicated; uncited.

147.verdict: Confirmed — DOPA in insect melanin chemistry
147.state: DOPA's role in insect cuticular melanin is well established: tyrosine hydroxylase and DOPA decarboxylase produce DOPA and dopamine for melanisation, the chemical sites of melanin deposition are mapped, and the pathway is genetically dissectable (DDC knockout causes albinism).
147.comparison: Przibram's study of DOPA's participation in cocoon melanin formation, and the localisation of melanisation's chemical sites, is an early contribution to the insect melanin-synthesis chemistry now fully mapped (tyrosinase, DOPA, DDC). Confirmed; uncited.

150.verdict: Now clinical — testicular autograft preserves spermatogenesis
150.state: Autologous testicular-tissue and spermatogonial-stem-cell transplantation is an active fertility-preservation strategy: grafted testicular tissue and stem cells maintain or restore spermatogenesis (now clinical for prepubertal cancer patients), with testicular organoids and in vitro spermatogenesis advancing.
150.comparison: Koppanyi's 1924 finding that spermatogenesis persists in an autoplastically transplanted testis — the organ retaining germinal function when moved — is the male counterpart to Wiesner's ovary graft and an early demonstration of exactly what underpins today's testicular-tissue autotransplantation for fertility preservation. Vindicated and now clinical; uncited.

153.verdict: Partly vindicated, partly refined — regeneration vs growth
153.state: The deep link between regeneration and growth is supported by modern biology — regeneration reactivates growth and developmental programmes, with shared signalling and proportional regrowth — though 'regeneration as merely accelerated normal growth' is a simplification: regeneration also adds distinct wound-response and dedifferentiation steps.
153.comparison: Przibram's thesis that regeneration is quantitatively a temporary acceleration of normal growth, unifying the two processes, captures a real and now-supported connection. Modern work both confirms the link and qualifies it — regeneration is more than accelerated growth. A foundational unifying idea, partly vindicated and partly refined. (Consensus retrieval here was noisy, matching 'regeneration' in forestry and governance.)

154.state: The mantis raptorial foreleg is studied evolutionarily (the walking-to-raptorial transition, with muscle and morphology changes) and as a homeotic subject; producing raptorial legs as homeotic regenerates fits the Hox and positional control of appendage identity.
154.comparison: Przibram's finding that raptorial legs can be produced as regenerates — a homeotic, heteromorphic outcome — is an early instance of appendage-identity transformation in the mantis, the very foreleg whose walking-to-raptorial evolution and developmental identity are now studied (and which Brecher cited in her 1924 antenna-foot work). Confirmed and explained in the Hox/evo-devo frame; uncited today.

158.verdict: Vindicated — tyrosinase is an O2-dependent oxidase
158.state: Tyrosinase biochemistry is well characterised: it is a copper-containing oxidase that requires molecular oxygen to convert tyrosine and DOPA toward melanin, so excluding air halts its activity; modern work also targets tyrosinase with inhibitors for hyperpigmentation.
158.comparison: Przibram and Dembowski's finding that excluding air conserves tyrosinase — the melanin-forming enzyme being oxygen-sensitive and preservable anaerobically — is an early, correct observation of tyrosinase's oxygen dependence (it is an O2-requiring copper oxidase). The biochemistry is vindicated; uncited.

160.state: This is another of Kammerer's neo-Lamarckian inheritance claims, now discredited; the legitimate modern descendant is epigenetic inheritance and developmental plasticity, with the heritability of enforced form-changes unconfirmed.
160.comparison: Kammerer's report that experimentally enforced form-changes were inherited is part of his discredited Lamarckian programme; modern biology rejects the specific claim while the general inheritance-of-acquired-traits question lives on, transformed, in epigenetics. Unconfirmed; reassessed as cautionary history.

161.verdict: Confirmed — Wolffian lens regeneration, now single-cell
161.state: Wolffian lens regeneration — the newt regrowing a lens from the dorsal iris by pigmented-epithelial-cell transdifferentiation — is a classic, now-molecular regeneration model: single-cell atlases of the regenerating iris, macrophage roles in fibrosis and inflammation, and the signalling of dorsal-iris competence are all characterised.
161.comparison: Uhlenhuth's study of the amphibian regenerating a new lens from the depigmenting dorsal iris is an early contribution to Wolffian lens regeneration — still a flagship transdifferentiation model, now dissected to single-cell resolution. His attention to dorsal-iris depigmentation and 'stimulus physiology' anticipates the modern mechanistic account; confirmed, uncited.

162.verdict: Echoed — ectopic lentoids inform lens competence
162.state: Lens formation and ectopic lentoid bodies are studied in development and regeneration: fish ocular organoids form lenses via alternative morphogenesis, and lentoid bodies (from non-lens cells) inform lens-fate competence.
162.comparison: Kolmer's documentation of a spontaneously formed second lens (lentoid) in a fish eye is an early natural-experiment observation bearing on lens-forming competence — exactly what modern lentoid and ocular-organoid work probes (cells beyond the lens can form lens tissue). A descriptive curiosity that prefigures lens-competence research; uncited.

163.verdict: Reopened — inheritance of acquired traits, now epigenetics
163.state: The inheritance of acquired characters — the theoretical crux of the institute's Lamarckian programme — is now reframed by epigenetics: some environmentally induced states can be transmitted via epigenetic marks, but this is bounded, mechanism-specific and distinct from classical Lamarckism, with the debate continuing.
163.comparison: Demoll's even-handed weighing of the case for and against the inheritance of acquired characters is exactly the question modern transgenerational epigenetics has reopened on new terms. His balanced stance reads as prescient: the answer is 'sometimes, by specific epigenetic means', not the wholesale Lamarckism the institute's experiments sought. A measured early treatment, uncited.

166.verdict: Descriptive value — intersex morphology and sex determination
166.state: Intersex and hermaphrodite anatomy informs sex-determination and sexual-dimorphism research: intersex individuals show intermediate skeletal sex indicators, and sex determination is increasingly mapped genetically and hormonally across taxa.
166.comparison: Stieve's documentation of the skeletal anatomy of a partial hermaphrodite — a natural intersex relevant to the sex-determination question — is an early descriptive contribution to intersex morphology, a phenomenon now used to probe how sex determination shapes the skeleton and how it can be intermediate. Descriptive value retained; uncited.

167.verdict: Reinterpreted — nuclear cycles and clonal size in ciliates
167.state: Ciliate nuclear reorganisation (conjugation and autogamy, the events once grouped as 'endomixis') and its physiological correlates are studied in Paramecium genetics, where the link between nuclear cycles and cell size, clonal vitality and clonal ageing remains of interest.
167.comparison: Erdmann's linking of body-size variation to the endomixis cycle in pure Paramecium lines is an early attempt to tie a nuclear-reorganisation rhythm to biometric variation and clonal vitality. The term 'endomixis' was later reinterpreted (as autogamy and macronuclear events), but the underlying question — nuclear cycles governing clonal size and vitality — persists. Partly superseded in terminology, conceptually alive; uncited.

168.state: Wolffian lens regeneration is now resolved to single-cell and signalling detail: studies identify all major newt-iris cell types during regeneration and the macrophage and inflammatory regulation of the iris-to-lens transdifferentiation.
168.comparison: Uhlenhuth's continued analysis of how the iris regenerates a lens — the conditions and tissue conversion of Wolffian regeneration — directly addresses the iris-to-lens transdifferentiation now mapped at single-cell resolution. Confirmed; uncited (companion to his 1919 study).

170.verdict: Confirmed — pupal colour chemistry, now pigment genetics
170.state: Environmentally cued pupal colour adaptation in butterflies is now understood at the level of pigment genetics and physiology: background and temperature modulate pupal melanisation, and the genes controlling black, blue and yellow pupal pigments have been identified, including in Pieris.
170.comparison: Brecher's sixth instalment, pinning down the 'chemism' — the pigment chemistry — behind the cabbage-white pupa's background-matched colour, is an early biochemical attack on what is now resolved as a pigment-pathway-and-environment system. His drive toward chemistry over mere description anticipates the modern molecular account; the phenomenon stands, his work uncited.

171.state: The tyrosinase–DOPA–melanin pathway Brecher examined is now textbook biochemistry and a major drug target: tyrosinase oxidises DOPA en route to melanin, and inhibiting it anchors skin-whitening, anti-browning and melanoma research, with the enzymatic steps reconstructed in vitro.
171.comparison: Brecher's demonstration that tyrosinase acts on DOPA to produce pigment identified the exact enzymatic step that underpins modern melanin biochemistry. The reaction he described is now a cornerstone — and a leading therapeutic and cosmetic target — yet the institute's contribution sits uncited beneath the vast literature it anticipated. Strongly confirmed.

1.verdict: Confirmed — partial-limb regeneration and positional information
1.state: Urodele limb regeneration is a premier model: newts and axolotls regrow limbs (and jaws, tendons) through a blastema governed by positional information, now mapped with single-cell, genomic and regeneration-specific gene-regulatory detail.
1.comparison: Abolins's study of how the newt restores a leg mutilated only at the fibular margin probes positional information in the blastema — how a partial wound rebuilds exactly what is missing — which is precisely the question modern molecular regeneration addresses (positional cues, blastema patterning). The phenomenon is central and confirmed; his specific paper barely cited.

2.verdict: Confirmed — positional rules of regenerative symmetry
2.state: The rules of regenerative symmetry — how axial and positional cues determine a regenerate's handedness and completeness — are now molecular: confronting dorsal and ventral blastema tissue triggers limb formation in axolotls, with WNT and FGF signalling setting the axes.
2.comparison: Burchhardt's finding that a regenerate's symmetry depends on how the limb is implanted into the body is an early demonstration of the positional-confrontation rules now formalised in axolotls (dorsoventral contact, with WNT10B and FGF2, drives outgrowth and symmetry). His grafting experiments prefigure modern positional-information regeneration; the principle holds, the paper near-uncited.

7.verdict: Echoed — homeotic mis-regeneration, now Hox identity
7.state: Heteromorphic and homeotic regeneration — regrowing the wrong structure at a wound site — is now understood through Hox-gene segmental identity and the endocrine (juvenile-hormone) control of insect regeneration and moulting.
7.comparison: Staudinger's catalogue of heteromorphoses at the stick insect's spiracles and other unexpected sites documents homeotic mis-regeneration — a regenerate taking on the wrong identity — which modern work explains through segmental Hox identity and hormonal control of insect regeneration. A descriptive widening of the phenomenon that maps onto today's gene-identity framework; essentially uncited.

13.verdict: Confirmed — temperature shapes extremity length (Allen's rule)
13.state: Temperature's control of body proportion — shorter extremities in the cold, as in Allen's rule — is now supported as a developmental and plastic effect: rearing temperature shapes extremity length, with epigenetic mediation and clear relevance to climate-driven body-size change.
13.comparison: This Przibram-school experiment — chemically cooling young rats and measuring the resulting shorter tails — is an early causal test of what is now framed as the developmental basis of Allen's rule: ambient temperature directly shapes extremity proportions, an effect modern work ties to plasticity and epigenetics. The causal claim is upheld by current thermal-developmental biology, though the specific paper is barely cited.

19.verdict: Echoed — mantis foreleg form and appendage regeneration
19.state: The mantis raptorial foreleg is an active subject of functional morphology — its form tuned by prey type, camouflage and phylogeny — while insect appendage regeneration is being resolved molecularly across species.
19.comparison: Thomson's comparative notes on how the raptorial foreleg regenerates across mantid genera braid together two threads still alive today: the foreleg as a much-studied adaptive structure and appendage regeneration as a molecular model. His cross-genus framing anticipates current evolutionary-morphology treatments of the mantis strike limb; descriptive value retained, citation negligible.

23.verdict: Historical marker — the institute's quantitative-biology programme
23.state: Quantitative biology — measuring and modelling life mathematically — has become a foundational mode across the sciences, from systems biology to biological-variation statistics, exactly the programme this catalogue announced.
23.comparison: This is not an experiment but a bibliography: Przibram gathering the institute's 1916–1919 quantitative-biology papers in English to advertise the programme's reach. Its interest today is historical — it documents how early and deliberately the Vivarium committed to a quantitative, measurement-driven biology, the very stance that now pervades the life sciences. No modern citation, but a revealing marker of the institute's ambition.

26.verdict: Prefigured — eye replantation, now regenerative ophthalmology
26.state: Eye and optic-nerve regeneration is intensely studied: fish and amphibians regenerate retina and (partially) optic nerve, and whole-eye transplantation is an active translational goal in mammals.
26.comparison: Koppanyi's third eye-replantation paper — showing a transplanted eye can survive and grow in fishes — is part of an institute series that prefigures both modern fish-eye regeneration research and the contemporary push toward whole-eye transplantation. The growth of a replanted eye he documented anticipates today's regenerative-ophthalmology agenda; the series remains largely uncited.

29.state: Amphibians' capacity to regenerate retina and recover vision — unlike mammals — is now a leading regeneration model, with the signalling pathways, transcription factors and epigenetics of retinal regrowth being mapped.
29.comparison: This instalment, reporting that the amphibian eye can be replanted and recover structure and some function, sits at the root of what is now a flagship finding: amphibians and fish regenerate functional retina where mammals cannot. The functional recovery Koppanyi and Kolmer noted is exactly the capacity modern molecular work is trying to reverse-engineer into mammals; confirmed, uncited.

30.verdict: Ahead of its time — mammalian whole-eye transplantation
30.state: Whole-eye transplantation in mammals — long thought impossible — is now an active frontier: the first human whole-eye transplant has been reported and large-animal models advance steadily, with optic-nerve regeneration the central remaining barrier.
30.comparison: Jellinek tackled the hardest case in the institute's eye series — replantation in mammals, where regenerative tolerance is least — and reached the same wall researchers face now: the mammalian eye can be transplanted, but restoring optic-nerve connection and vision is the obstacle. A century on, whole-eye transplantation is real yet vision-restoration still pending, making his framing strikingly current; uncited.

31.state: Insect tissue grafting and appendage regeneration are well established and now molecular, but Finkler's specific claim — that whole heads could be exchanged (even between sexes) with lasting survival and functional integration — was contentious in its day and has not been substantiated.
31.comparison: Finkler's head-transplantation reports were among the institute's most sensational and most disputed; this instalment, swapping Hydrophilus heads between sexes, pushed the claim furthest. Modern insect science readily grafts tissues and dissects appendage regeneration, but offers no support for functional whole-head exchange between sexes — the dramatic version of the claim did not survive scrutiny. A cautionary, largely superseded episode.
